schools.summary: Gill: the tabernacle was a habitation for God to dwell in, made of ten curtains of fine twined linen, blue, purple, and scarlet, with cherubim of cunning work; the witnesses note God commanding the tabernacle to be made of ten curtains of fine twined linen and the three colours, wrought with cherubim — the inner covering of the tabernacle, rich and beautiful, the dwelling-place of God among his people (God appointing a costly and figured habitation: 'thou shalt make the tabernacle with ten curtains of fine twined linen, and blue, and purple, and scarlet: with cherubims of cunning work'; the whole, that the tabernacle was a habitation for God to dwell in among his people, a type of God dwelling with men; the materials, ten curtains of fine twined linen, and blue, and purple, and scarlet, the richest and finest stuff, in colours of heavenly and royal worth; the figures, cherubims of cunning work, that the curtains were embroidered with cherubim, the angelic ministers, woven by the skill of the craftsman; the typology the commentators draw, that the tabernacle figures Christ, in whom God dwells bodily and who tabernacled among us, and the church, the colours and the linen shadowing his graces and righteousness; that God would dwell among his people in a fit and glorious habitation).

sides.position: God dwelling with men — a type of Christ, in whom God dwells bodily and who 'tabernacled' among us (John 1:14), and of the church; the fine linen and colours shadowing his graces and righteousness

themes: thou shalt make the tabernacle with ten curtains of fine twined linen, and blue, and purple, and scarlet
with cherubims of cunning work shalt thou make them
the inner covering of the tabernacle, God's dwelling-place among his people
a type of Christ, in whom God dwells bodily and who tabernacled among us

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: the length of one curtain shall be eight and twenty cubits, and the breadth four cubits, every one of one measure; the witnesses note God appointing each curtain to be twenty-eight cubits long and four broad, all of one measure — the exact and uniform measure of the curtains, the order and proportion of God's house (God fixing the precise size of each piece: 'The length of one curtain shall be eight and twenty cubits, and the breadth of one curtain four cubits: and every one of the curtains shall have one measure'; the dimensions, eight and twenty cubits long, about fourteen yards, and four cubits broad, the size of each of the ten linen curtains; the uniformity, 'every one of the curtains shall have one measure,' that all ten were exactly alike, of one size, that they might join evenly and form one whole; the order and exactness the commentators mark, that nothing in God's house was left to chance or fancy but all made by precise measure after the pattern shown in the mount; that God is a God of order, and his house is built by rule and proportion; the lesson, that the church, God's spiritual house, is fitly framed together).

themes: The length of one curtain shall be eight and twenty cubits, and the breadth of one curtain four cubits
and every one of the curtains shall have one measure
each curtain of exact and uniform measure, all alike that they might join into one whole
nothing in God's house left to chance, but all by precise measure after the pattern, for God is a God of order

schools.summary: Gill: five of the ten curtains were to be sewed together and make as it were one curtain; the witnesses note God appointing that five curtains be coupled together, and the other five likewise, into two great pieces — the joining of the curtains into two, soon to be made one (the ten first made two: 'The five curtains shall be coupled together one to another; and other five curtains shall be coupled one to another'; the joining, that five of the ten curtains were to be sewed together into one large curtain, and the other five into another, making two great pieces of five each; that these two great curtains were then to be coupled by loops and taches into one whole tabernacle covering; the order in the work, that the parts were first joined in twos and then made one, the many becoming one; the lesson the commentators draw, of the joining together of the many into one, as the saints are knit together and the two peoples, Jew and Gentile, made one in Christ; that God's house is made of many parts fitly joined).

themes: The five curtains shall be coupled together one to another
and other five curtains shall be coupled one to another
the ten curtains joined into two great pieces of five each, soon to be made one
a figure of the saints knit together, and Jew and Gentile made one in Christ

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: the loops were made from the selvedge in the coupling, along the edge where the two great curtains were to be joined; the witnesses note God appointing loops of blue on the edge of the one great curtain and likewise on the other, where they were to be coupled — the loops by which the two great curtains were to be joined (the means of joining appointed: 'thou shalt make loops of blue upon the edge of the one curtain... and likewise... in the uttermost edge of another curtain, in the coupling of the second'; the loops, 'loops of blue upon the edge of the one curtain,' small loops of blue thread sewn along the selvedge, the finished edge, of the one great curtain; the matching, 'likewise... in the uttermost edge of another curtain,' that the same loops were set on the edge of the other great curtain, exactly answering, that the two might be joined; the care and exactness, that the loops were placed in the coupling, just where the two were to meet, each answering the other; that God appoints not only the parts of his house but the very means by which they are joined, all in order; that the joining of the parts was provided for beforehand, that the whole might be one).

themes: thou shalt make loops of blue upon the edge of the one curtain from the selvedge in the coupling
in the uttermost edge of another curtain, in the coupling of the second
the loops of blue made on the matching edges where the two great curtains were to be joined
God appointing not only the parts of his house but the very means by which they are joined

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: fifty loops shalt thou make in the one curtain, and fifty in the edge of the other, that the loops may take hold one of another; the witnesses note God appointing fifty loops on each of the two edges, so set that the loops might take hold one of another — the exact answering of the loops, that the two curtains might join perfectly (fifty loops answering to fifty: 'Fifty loops shalt thou make in the one curtain, and fifty loops shalt thou make in the edge of the curtain that is in the coupling of the second; that the loops may take hold one of another'; the number, fifty loops on the edge of each great curtain, the same number on both; the exact placing, that the loops were set that the loops may take hold one of another, each loop on the one edge answering exactly to a loop on the other, so that the taches might pass through both and join them; the perfect correspondence, that nothing was awry, every loop answering its fellow, the two edges made to fit exactly; the order and care of God in his house, that all the parts answer one another and join without flaw; the lesson, that in God's spiritual house all the members are fitly joined, each answering its place).

themes: Fifty loops shalt thou make in the one curtain, and fifty loops shalt thou make in the edge of the curtain that is in the coupling of the second
that the loops may take hold one of another
fifty loops answering fifty, each set to take hold of its fellow
the order and care of God, that all the parts of his house answer one another and join without flaw

schools.summary: Gill: the taches, which some render buttons, others hooks, seem to be clasps, by which the curtains were coupled together; the witnesses note God appointing fifty taches of gold to couple the two great curtains together, that it might be one tabernacle — the golden clasps that made the two one, the unity of God's house (the parts made one by the golden taches: 'thou shalt make fifty taches of gold, and couple the curtains together with the taches: and it shall be one tabernacle'; the means, fifty taches of gold, fifty golden clasps or hooks, one for each pair of answering loops, by which the two great curtains were joined; the joining, 'couple the curtains together with the taches,' that the taches, passing through the loops, drew and held the two edges together; the great result, 'and it shall be one tabernacle,' that, though made of ten curtains and two great pieces, the whole became one tabernacle, one dwelling-place, the many made one; the lesson the commentators draw, of the unity of the church, made one in Christ by the golden bond of love and the Spirit, the many members one body; that what God joins is one, fitly framed and held together).

themes: thou shalt make fifty taches of gold, and couple the curtains together with the taches
and it shall be one tabernacle
the fifty golden taches coupling the two great curtains into one tabernacle
a figure of the church made one in Christ by the golden bond of love and the Spirit

schools.summary: Gill: the goats' hair, the softer and finer part, was spun by women and made into a stuff like camelot; these curtains were a covering over the linen ones; the witnesses note God appointing eleven curtains of goats' hair to be a covering upon the tabernacle — the second covering, of goats' hair, set over the rich linen curtains (God appointing a tent of goats' hair above the dwelling: 'thou shalt make curtains of goats' hair to be a covering upon the tabernacle: eleven curtains shalt thou make'; the material, 'curtains of goats' hair,' made of the fine, soft hair of goats, spun by the women into a strong stuff like camelot, coarser than the linen; the use, 'to be a covering upon the tabernacle,' that these curtains formed a tent over the inner linen curtains, to cover and shelter the dwelling; the number, eleven curtains, one more than the linen ten, that they might reach further and cover the whole; the type some commentators draw, that the plain goats'-hair covering, set over the rich and figured linen, may shadow the lowly outward appearance that hid the inner glory, as in Christ, who had no outward beauty yet was full of grace within; that God's house had a covering of strength and shelter over its beauty).

themes: thou shalt make curtains of goats' hair to be a covering upon the tabernacle
eleven curtains shalt thou make
the goats'-hair curtains set as a covering over the rich linen ones
some seeing it shadow the lowly outward appearance that hid the inner glory, as in Christ

schools.summary: Gill: the breadth was the same with the linen curtains, but the length two cubits more, that they might hang down lower; the witnesses note God appointing each goats'-hair curtain to be thirty cubits long and four broad, all of one measure — the larger size of the outer curtains, that they might hang lower and cover all (the covering made to reach further: 'The length of one curtain shall be thirty cubits, and the breadth of one curtain four cubits: and the eleven curtains shall be all of one measure'; the dimensions, thirty cubits long, two cubits more than the linen's twenty-eight, and four cubits broad, the same as the linen; the reason for the greater length, that the goats'-hair curtains, being the outer covering, were made two cubits more in length that they might hang down lower than the inner curtains, covering and overlapping them; the uniformity, 'the eleven curtains shall be all of one measure,' all alike, as the linen, that they might join evenly; the same order and exactness as before, every piece by precise measure after the pattern; that the outer covering was made to overhang and shelter the inner, the larger protecting the rich within).

themes: The length of one curtain shall be thirty cubits, and the breadth of one curtain four cubits
and the eleven curtains shall be all of one measure
the goats'-hair curtains made larger, that as the outer covering they might hang lower
all of one measure by precise rule, the larger protecting the rich within

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: the additional curtain was doubled back in the forefront of the tabernacle, the sixth folded over the entrance; the witnesses note God appointing five goats'-hair curtains coupled by themselves and six by themselves, with the sixth doubled in the forefront — the joining of the outer covering, with a doubled fold at the entrance (the eleven joined, five and six: 'thou shalt couple five curtains by themselves, and six curtains by themselves, and shalt double the sixth curtain in the forefront of the tabernacle'; the joining, 'five curtains by themselves, and six curtains by themselves,' that, as with the linen, the goats'-hair curtains were made into two great pieces, but unevenly, five in the one and six in the other, the eleventh being the odd one; the doubled fold, 'shalt double the sixth curtain in the forefront of the tabernacle,' that the extra sixth curtain of the front piece was doubled back and folded over in the forefront, at the entrance, hanging double there; the care for the front, that the entrance, most exposed, was given a double covering for strength and seemliness; the exactness of God's appointment, even to the folding of a curtain).

themes: thou shalt couple five curtains by themselves, and six curtains by themselves
and shalt double the sixth curtain in the forefront of the tabernacle
the goats'-hair curtains joined five and six, the sixth doubled over the entrance
a double covering at the exposed front, the exactness of God's appointment even to the folding of a curtain

schools.summary: Gill: the loops were put in like manner and in the same place as on the linen curtains, only these are not said to be of blue; the witnesses note God appointing fifty loops on the edge of each great goats'-hair curtain, for the joining — the loops for the coupling of the outer covering, as with the inner (the same provision made for joining the goats'-hair: 'thou shalt make fifty loops on the edge of the one curtain that is outmost in the coupling, and fifty loops in the edge of the curtain which coupleth the second'; the loops, fifty loops on the edge of the one curtain and fifty in the edge of the second, that, just as with the linen curtains, fifty loops were set on each of the two great goats'-hair pieces where they were to be coupled; the difference noted, that these loops are not said to be of blue, as the linen's were, the outer covering being plainer; the same exact correspondence, the loops answering one to another, that the two might join; the order and consistency of God's appointment, the outer covering joined by the same method as the inner; that even the plainer parts of God's house were made with the same care).

themes: thou shalt make fifty loops on the edge of the one curtain that is outmost in the coupling
and fifty loops in the edge of the curtain which coupleth the second
fifty loops set on each edge of the goats'-hair curtains for the coupling, as with the linen
the same exact care, even the plainer parts of God's house made by the same method

schools.summary: Gill: as these curtains were coarser, so the taches were made of meaner metal, brass, but answered the purpose of joining full as well; the witnesses note God appointing fifty taches of brass to join the goats'-hair curtains, that the tent might be one — the brass clasps that made the outer covering one, of meaner metal than the golden (the goats'-hair made one by brazen taches: 'thou shalt make fifty taches of brass, and put the taches into the loops, and couple the tent together, that it may be one'; the means, 'fifty taches of brass,' brazen clasps, not golden as for the linen, because these curtains were coarser, the meaner metal befitting the plainer covering, yet answering the purpose full as well; the joining, 'put the taches into the loops, and couple the tent together,' that the brass taches, passing through the loops, drew the two great pieces together; the result, 'that it may be one,' that the goats'-hair covering, though of eleven curtains, became one tent; the fitness the commentators mark, that the inner and richer was joined with gold, the outer and plainer with brass, each according to its place; that all the parts, rich or plain, were made one by God's appointment).

themes: thou shalt make fifty taches of brass, and put the taches into the loops
and couple the tent together, that it may be one
the fifty brass taches joining the goats'-hair curtains into one tent
of meaner metal than the golden taches, befitting the plainer covering, each part according to its place

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: even after the doubling back, the goats'-hair covering would be half a curtain longer, which was to hang over the backside; the witnesses note God appointing that the remnant, the half curtain that remained of the goats'-hair, should hang over the backside of the tabernacle — the disposing of the surplus length, the extra hanging down behind (the overplus appointed its place: 'the remnant that remaineth of the curtains of the tent, the half curtain that remaineth, shall hang over the backside of the tabernacle'; the surplus, that the goats'-hair curtains, being one more in number and longer than the linen, had a remnant left over after covering the dwelling; the disposing, 'the half curtain that remaineth, shall hang over the backside,' that this extra half curtain was to hang down over the back, the west end, of the tabernacle, covering it; the care of God for every part, even the surplus given its place and use, nothing wasted or left disordered; the exactness of the pattern, that even the overplus was provided for in the design; that God orders all things in his house, even to the least remnant).

themes: the remnant that remaineth of the curtains of the tent, the half curtain that remaineth
shall hang over the backside of the tabernacle
the surplus half curtain appointed to hang over the back of the tabernacle
every part given its place and use, God ordering all in his house even to the least remnant

schools.summary: Gill: the goats'-hair curtains were two cubits longer than the linen, and these are the two cubits, a cubit hanging over on each side to cover the tabernacle; the witnesses note God appointing a cubit on each side of the surplus length to hang over the sides of the tabernacle and cover it — the overhanging of the outer covering on both sides, sheltering the dwelling (the extra length covering the sides: 'a cubit on the one side, and a cubit on the other side... it shall hang over the sides of the tabernacle on this side and on that side, to cover it'; the surplus in length, that the goats'-hair curtains being two cubits longer than the linen, there was a cubit of overhang on the one side and a cubit on the other; the use, 'it shall hang over the sides of the tabernacle... to cover it,' that this extra cubit on each side hung down over the sides of the dwelling, covering and protecting the inner curtains and the boards; the completeness of the covering, that the outer tent overhung the inner on the back and both sides, sheltering the whole; the care of God that his dwelling be fully covered and protected on every side; that nothing of the holy place was left exposed).

themes: a cubit on the one side, and a cubit on the other side of that which remaineth in the length of the curtains of the tent
it shall hang over the sides of the tabernacle on this side and on that side, to cover it
the extra cubit on each side hanging over the sides to cover the tabernacle
the care of God that his dwelling be fully covered and protected on every side, nothing left exposed

schools.summary: Gill: this was a covering put over the curtains of goats' hair, of rams' skins dyed red, and over that a covering of badgers' skins, to keep out the rain; the witnesses note God appointing a covering of rams' skins dyed red, and above it a covering of badgers' skins — the two outer protective coverings, plain without but full of meaning (two more coverings sheltering the whole: 'thou shalt make a covering for the tent of rams' skins dyed red, and a covering above of badgers' skins'; the third covering, 'rams' skins dyed red,' the skins of rams dyed red, set over the goats'-hair, a covering of strength; the type the commentators draw, that the rams' skins dyed red shadow the sufferings and atoning blood of Christ, the Ram offered in our stead, his consecration and death; the fourth and outermost covering, 'badgers' skins,' or seal or dugong skins, a plain, weather-proof covering to keep out the rain, rough and unlovely without; the type drawn, that the plain badgers' skins outermost shadow Christ's lowly outward appearance, who, seen from without, hath no form nor comeliness, the glory being hidden within; the order, from the rich figured linen within to the plain skins without, the glory inmost).

debates.question: What do the rams' skins dyed red and the badgers' skins typify?

sides.position: The rams' skins dyed red shadow the sufferings and atoning blood of Christ, the Ram offered in our stead; the plain, outermost badgers' skins shadow Christ's lowly outward appearance ('no form nor comeliness,' Isaiah 53:2), the glory hidden within

themes: thou shalt make a covering for the tent of rams' skins dyed red
and a covering above of badgers' skins
the two outermost coverings, plain without but full of meaning
the rams' skins dyed red shadowing Christ's atoning blood, the badgers' skins his lowly outward appearance

schools.summary: Gill: the boards were the pillars and supports, and properly the walls of the tabernacle, which gave it its firmness and security; the witnesses note God commanding boards of shittim wood, standing up, to be made for the tabernacle — the upright frame of the dwelling, its walls and support (God appointing a firm wooden frame to bear the curtains: 'thou shalt make boards for the tabernacle of shittim wood standing up'; the use, that the boards were the pillars and supports, and properly the walls of the tabernacle, giving it firmness and security, that the curtains might be stretched upon a solid frame; the material, shittim wood, the acacia, an incorruptible and durable wood, of which the boards were made and afterward overlaid with gold; the posture, 'standing up,' that the boards stood upright, the walls reared on end; the type the commentators draw, that the boards, of incorruptible wood overlaid with gold, set upright and firm, may shadow the saints, or the humanity and deity of Christ, upright and standing in God's house, made firm and incorruptible by his grace; that God's house is a stable and enduring frame).

themes: thou shalt make boards for the tabernacle of shittim wood
standing up
the boards of incorruptible shittim wood, set upright, the firm walls of the tabernacle
some seeing them shadow the saints, upright and standing in God's house, made firm by his grace

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: ten cubits shall be the length of a board, and a cubit and a half the breadth of one board; the witnesses note God appointing each board to be ten cubits long and a cubit and a half broad — the exact and uniform measure of the boards, the height and breadth of the walls (God fixing the size of each board: 'Ten cubits shall be the length of a board, and a cubit and a half shall be the breadth of one board'; the height, ten cubits, about five yards, the length of each board, which, standing upright, gave the tabernacle its height; the breadth, a cubit and a half broad each board, so that a number set side by side made the length of the walls; the uniformity, that all the boards were of one measure, alike, that the walls might be even and the boards join closely; the same order and exactness as in the curtains, every board by precise measure after the pattern; that God's house was built throughout by rule and proportion, nothing left to chance; the lesson, that all in God's spiritual house is fitly framed, each member of due measure).

themes: Ten cubits shall be the length of a board
and a cubit and a half shall be the breadth of one board
each board of exact and uniform measure, the height and breadth of the walls
all built by rule and proportion after the pattern, nothing left to chance

schools.summary: Gill: every board was shaped at the lower end to have, as it were, two hands, as in the original, to enter into and lay hold of the sockets; the witnesses note God appointing two tenons on each board, set in order one against another, for all the boards — the projecting tenons by which the boards were fixed in the sockets (each board having two hands to hold its place: 'Two tenons shall there be in one board, set in order one against another: thus shalt thou make for all the boards'; the tenons, 'two tenons... in one board,' two projections at the foot of each board, called in the Hebrew two hands, cut to enter and lay hold of the sockets below; the order, 'set in order one against another,' the two tenons evenly placed, side by side, answering each to its socket; the uniformity, 'thus shalt thou make for all the boards,' every board alike, each with its two tenons; the firmness given thereby, that each board, held by its two tenons in two sockets, stood fast and could not slip; the lesson the commentators draw, that as each board was held firm by its tenons in its silver sockets, so the saints are held fast and established, each in his place, by the grace of God).

themes: Two tenons shall there be in one board, set in order one against another
thus shalt thou make for all the boards of the tabernacle
the two tenons ('two hands') by which each board laid hold of the sockets and stood fast
a figure of the saints held fast and established, each in his place, by the grace of God

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: thou shalt make the boards for the tabernacle, twenty boards on the south side southward; the witnesses note God appointing twenty boards for the south side of the tabernacle — the first wall of the dwelling, the south side reckoned (God numbering the boards of each side: 'thou shalt make the boards for the tabernacle, twenty boards on the south side southward'; the number, twenty boards for the south side, each a cubit and a half broad, making the length of that wall thirty cubits; the side, 'on the south side southward,' the long south wall of the tabernacle, the first of the sides reckoned; the order of the account, that God appoints the boards side by side, beginning with the south, then the north, then the west; the exactness of the design, the precise number for each wall, that the frame might be just and complete; that God orders his house by number and measure, each part in its place; the lesson, that in God's house every member is numbered and set in his place).

themes: And thou shalt make the boards for the tabernacle
twenty boards on the south side southward
the twenty boards appointed for the south wall of the tabernacle
God ordering his house by number and measure, each part in its place

schools.summary: Gill: the sockets, or bases, were properly the foundation of the tabernacle, on which it was settled and established, two under each board for its two tenons; the witnesses note God appointing forty silver sockets under the twenty boards, two under each board for its two tenons — the silver foundation of the tabernacle, on which the boards stood fast (the boards settled on a foundation of silver: 'thou shalt make forty sockets of silver under the twenty boards; two sockets under one board for his two tenons'; the sockets, forty sockets of silver, two for each of the twenty boards, which were properly the foundation of the tabernacle, on which it was settled and established; the fitting, 'two sockets under one board for his two tenons,' that each board's two tenons were set into two silver sockets, as into mortises, that it might stand firm; the material, that the foundation was of silver, the metal of the atonement money, the redemption-silver, so that the whole house rested on silver; the type the commentators draw, that the silver sockets, made of the redemption-money, shadow Christ's redemption as the foundation on which the church stands and is established; that God's house rests on a sure and precious foundation).

debates.question: What do the silver sockets, the foundation, signify?

sides.position: Christ's redemption — the sockets being made of silver, the metal of the atonement/redemption-money (Exodus 30:11-16); the whole house resting on silver shadows the church standing and established on the foundation of Christ's redemption

themes: thou shalt make forty sockets of silver under the twenty boards
two sockets under one board for his two tenons
the forty silver sockets, the foundation on which the tabernacle was settled
the silver, the atonement-money, shadowing Christ's redemption as the foundation on which the church stands

schools.summary: Gill: the direction of the tabernacle was east and west; at the east end was the entrance, at the west end the most holy place; the north was the second side; the witnesses note God appointing twenty boards for the north side of the tabernacle, the second wall — the matching of the second long wall to the first (the north answering the south: 'for the second side of the tabernacle on the north side there shall be twenty boards'; the number, twenty boards for the north side, exactly as for the south, the two long walls alike; the orientation, that the tabernacle stood east and west, the entrance at the east, the most holy at the west, the two long sides being north and south; that the north wall, like the south, was of twenty boards, the same length and frame, the two sides answering each other; the order and symmetry of God's house, the sides matched and even; that God's dwelling was built in just proportion, balanced on every side; the lesson, that all is order and proportion in God's house).

themes: And for the second side of the tabernacle on the north side
there shall be twenty boards
the twenty boards for the north wall, matching the south, the two long sides alike
the order and symmetry of God's house, built in just proportion, balanced on every side

schools.summary: Gill: the forty sockets answerable to the twenty boards, for their two tenons to be placed in as in mortises, two sockets under each board; the witnesses note God appointing forty silver sockets for the north boards also, two under each — the like silver foundation under the second wall (the north wall too resting on silver: 'their forty sockets of silver; two sockets under one board, and two sockets under another board'; the number, forty sockets of silver for the north, the same as for the south, two under each of the twenty boards; the fitting, 'two sockets under one board, and two sockets under another,' each board's two tenons set in two sockets, as in mortises, that it might stand firm; the sameness, that the north wall rested on the same silver foundation as the south, the whole house on silver; the type as before, that the silver foundation, of the redemption-money, shadows Christ's redemption, on which the whole church, on every side, is established; that every part of God's house rests on the one foundation; the lesson, that all the saints, on every side, stand on the same foundation of Christ).

themes: And their forty sockets of silver
two sockets under one board, and two sockets under another board
the forty silver sockets under the north boards also, the whole house resting on silver
the silver, the redemption-money, shadowing Christ's redemption, on which the whole church is established

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: 'for the sides of the tabernacle westward,' rather for the back or hinder part, the west end being the back; the witnesses note God appointing six boards for the west side of the tabernacle, the back or hinder end — the third wall, the shorter west end (the back of the dwelling framed: 'for the sides of the tabernacle westward thou shalt make six boards'; the side, 'the sides of the tabernacle westward,' better the back or hinder part, the west end of the tabernacle, which was the back, the most holy place being there; the number, six boards, fewer than the long sides, for the west was the short end, six boards making, with the corners, the breadth of the tabernacle; that the west end, being the back behind the most holy place, was closed and walled, having no entrance, the door being at the east; the order of the account, the south and north, the long sides, reckoned first, then the west, the back; the completeness, that every side of the dwelling was framed and closed save the entrance; the exactness of the design throughout).

themes: And for the sides of the tabernacle westward
thou shalt make six boards
the six boards for the west end, the back of the tabernacle behind the most holy place
the back closed and walled with no entrance, every side framed save the door at the east

schools.summary: Gill: two boards for the corners of the tabernacle, for the northwest and southwest corners, in the north and south sides; the witnesses note God appointing two boards for the corners of the tabernacle in the two sides — the corner boards that joined and strengthened the back to the sides (the corners framed and bound: 'two boards shalt thou make for the corners of the tabernacle in the two sides'; the corner boards, 'two boards... for the corners,' set at the northwest and southwest corners, where the west end met the two long sides; the use, that these corner boards joined the back wall to the side walls, binding the frame together at the angles and giving it strength; the care of God for the joining of the parts, even the corners framed and bound, that the whole might stand fast; the exactness of the design, that nothing was left loose or unjoined, the corners as well as the walls provided for; the lesson the commentators draw, that in God's house the parts are bound and knit together at every point, the corners as the walls).

themes: And two boards shalt thou make for the corners of the tabernacle
in the two sides
the two corner boards joining the back wall to the sides and binding the frame at the angles
the care of God for the joining of the parts, even the corners framed and bound, that the whole might stand fast

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: the corner boards were to be coupled to the adjoining boards beneath and above, fitted into one ring at the top; the witnesses note God appointing that the corner boards be coupled together beneath and above, to one ring, for both corners — the close binding of the corner boards top and bottom (the corners firmly knit at every point: 'they shall be coupled together beneath, and... above the head of it unto one ring: thus shall it be for them both; they shall be for the two corners'; the coupling below, 'coupled together beneath,' at the bottom of the boards, where they met; the coupling above, 'coupled together above the head of it unto one ring,' at the top, both boards fitted into one ring, binding them fast at the head; the sameness, 'thus shall it be for them both,' that the same was done at each corner, the two corners, northwest and southwest; the firmness, that the corner boards, bound at top and bottom, held the frame tight and square; the lesson the commentators draw, that God's house is knit together at every joint, top and bottom, the parts compacted into one; that what God frames is firmly and wholly joined).

themes: they shall be coupled together beneath, and... above the head of it unto one ring
thus shall it be for them both; they shall be for the two corners
the corner boards coupled beneath and above to one ring, bound fast top and bottom
God's house knit together at every joint, the parts compacted into one

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: sixteen sockets, two for each corner board, and twelve for the six boards, eight boards in all at the west; the witnesses note God appointing eight boards in all for the west end, with sixteen silver sockets, two under each — the full reckoning of the west wall, boards and foundation (the back wholly framed and founded: 'they shall be eight boards, and their sockets of silver, sixteen sockets; two sockets under one board, and two sockets under another'; the number, eight boards for the west end, the six boards and the two corner boards together making eight; the sockets, 'their sockets of silver, sixteen sockets,' two under each of the eight boards, as for the other walls, the west also founded on silver; the fitting, 'two sockets under one board, and two sockets under another,' each board's tenons set in two silver sockets, firm as the rest; that the west wall, like the south and north, rested wholly on the silver foundation, the whole house on silver; the type as before, the silver foundation shadowing Christ's redemption, on which every part of the church is established; that God's house is complete and founded on every side).

themes: And they shall be eight boards, and their sockets of silver, sixteen sockets
two sockets under one board, and two sockets under another board
the eight boards and sixteen silver sockets of the west end, the back also founded on silver
the whole house on the silver foundation, shadowing Christ's redemption on which every part of the church is established

schools.summary: Gill: the bars, being put into rings or staples of gold, kept the boards tight, close, and firm together; the witnesses note God appointing bars of shittim wood, five for the boards of the one side — the bars that bound the upright boards into a firm wall (God providing bars to bind the boards fast: 'thou shalt make bars of shittim wood; five for the boards of the one side of the tabernacle'; the bars, 'bars of shittim wood,' long poles of the same incorruptible wood as the boards, that ran along the walls; the use, that the bars, put into rings or staples of gold, kept the boards tight, close, and firm together, binding the upright boards into one solid wall; the number, 'five for the boards of the one side,' five bars to each long side, holding all the boards of that wall; the firmness given, that the boards, though many, were bound by the bars into one and could not be shaken; the lesson the commentators draw, that as the bars bound the boards into one firm wall, so the saints are bound together and held firm by the bond of love and the Spirit; that God's house is not only founded but bound and braced).

themes: thou shalt make bars of shittim wood
five for the boards of the one side of the tabernacle
the bars of shittim wood that bound the upright boards into one firm wall
a figure of the saints bound together and held firm by the bond of love and the Spirit

schools.summary: Gill: the bars for the north side were disposed in the same manner as those for the south, and likewise for the west end; the witnesses note God appointing five bars also for the other long side and for the west end — the same binding for every wall, that all might be firm (each side bound by its bars: 'five bars for the boards of the other side... and five bars for the boards of the side of the tabernacle, for the two sides westward'; the second side, 'five bars for the boards of the other side,' that the north wall, like the south, was bound by five bars; the west end, 'five bars for the boards... for the two sides westward,' that the back wall too had its bars, holding its boards firm; the sameness, that every wall, south, north, and west, was bound alike by five bars, all made firm by the same means; the completeness, that no wall was left unbound, the whole frame braced on every side; the lesson, that in God's house every part is alike bound and held firm, none left loose; that God braces his dwelling on every side).

themes: five bars for the boards of the other side of the tabernacle
and five bars for the boards of the side of the tabernacle, for the two sides westward
five bars also for the north wall and the west end, every wall bound alike
no wall left unbound, the whole frame braced on every side, none left loose

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: the middle bar in the midst of the boards shall reach from end to end, from east to west; the witnesses note God appointing the middle bar to run through the midst of the boards, reaching from end to end — the one long hidden bar that ran the whole length and bound all (one bar reaching through the whole wall: 'the middle bar in the midst of the boards shall reach from end to end'; the place, 'in the midst of the boards,' that this bar ran, not on the surface like the others, which lay in golden rings, but through the very middle of the boards, hidden within them; the length, 'shall reach from end to end,' from east to west, the one bar running the whole length of the wall and binding all the boards from one end to the other; the distinction, that, while the other bars were shorter and several, this middle bar was one and entire, passing through the heart of the boards; the type the commentators draw, that this one hidden middle bar, running the whole length and binding all, may shadow Christ, the secret and inward bond who, unseen, holds the whole house together, or the Spirit who knits all the members into one; that there is one inward bond that holds God's house entire).

debates.question: What does the one hidden middle bar 'from end to end' shadow?

sides.position: Christ, the secret and inward bond who, unseen, holds the whole house together — or the Spirit who knits all the members into one; the one entire inward bond that holds God's house entire

themes: And the middle bar in the midst of the boards
shall reach from end to end
the one hidden middle bar running through the midst of the boards, binding the whole wall
some seeing it shadow Christ, the secret inward bond who unseen holds the whole house together

schools.summary: Gill: the boards were not merely gilded but covered with thin plates of gold, and the bars likewise overlaid with gold; the witnesses note God commanding the boards and bars to be overlaid with gold, with rings of gold to hold the bars — the gilding of the whole frame, the wood covered with glory (the shittim wood clothed in gold: 'thou shalt overlay the boards with gold, and make their rings of gold for places for the bars: and thou shalt overlay the bars with gold'; the boards, 'overlay the boards with gold,' not merely gilded but covered with thin plates of gold, the incorruptible wood wholly clothed in gold; the rings, 'their rings of gold for places for the bars,' golden rings fixed on the boards, through which the bars passed; the bars, 'overlay the bars with gold,' that the bars too were covered with gold; that all that was seen within the holy place was gold, the whole frame shining; the type the commentators draw, that the wood overlaid with gold may shadow the two natures of Christ, the humanity, the incorruptible wood, clothed with the deity and glory, the gold, or the saints, by nature wood, clothed with the gold of his righteousness and glory; that God clothes his house, and his people, with glory).

debates.question: What does the shittim wood overlaid with gold shadow?

sides.position: The two natures of Christ — the humanity (the incorruptible wood) clothed with the deity and glory (the gold); or the saints, by nature wood, clothed with the gold of his righteousness and glory

themes: thou shalt overlay the boards with gold, and make their rings of gold for places for the bars
and thou shalt overlay the bars with gold
the boards and bars overlaid with gold, the incorruptible wood wholly clothed in glory
some seeing the two natures of Christ, or the saints clothed with the gold of his righteousness

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: 'according to the fashion thereof,' after the pattern which was shown to Moses in the mount; the witnesses note God charging Moses to rear up the tabernacle according to the fashion shown him in the mount — the great rule, that all be made after the heavenly pattern (nothing done but after the pattern: 'thou shalt rear up the tabernacle according to the fashion thereof which was shewed thee in the mount'; the charge, 'thou shalt rear up the tabernacle,' that, when all the parts were made, Moses should set up the whole, putting each in its place; the rule, 'according to the fashion thereof which was shewed thee in the mount,' that all must be made and set up exactly after the pattern God had shown Moses on Sinai, nothing added, altered, or left out; the great principle the commentators draw, that in the worship of God nothing is to be done by man's invention, but all according to the pattern, the will of God being the only rule; that the tabernacle and its pattern were shadows of heavenly things, and so the more to be exactly followed; that God will be served his own way, not ours).

debates.question: What does 'according to the fashion shewed thee in the mount' teach about worship?

sides.position: That in the worship of God nothing is to be done by man's invention, but all according to the pattern — the will of God the only rule (Hebrews 8:5); the tabernacle being a shadow of heavenly things, the more to be exactly followed, for God will be served his own way, not ours

themes: thou shalt rear up the tabernacle according to the fashion thereof
which was shewed thee in the mount
the charge to rear up the tabernacle exactly after the pattern shown in the mount
in worship nothing by man's invention, but all according to the pattern, the will of God the only rule

schools.summary: Gill: the use of the veil was to divide the holy place from the most holy in the tabernacle; it was made of the same rich materials and figures as the inner curtains; the witnesses note God commanding a veil of blue, purple, scarlet, and fine twined linen, of cunning work, with cherubim — the rich veil that divided the holy from the most holy (the veil wrought as the curtains, with cherubim: 'thou shalt make a vail of blue, and purple, and scarlet, and fine twined linen of cunning work: with cherubims shall it be made'; the materials, blue, purple, scarlet, and fine twined linen, the same rich stuff and colours as the inner curtains, the costliest and most beautiful; the figures, 'with cherubims shall it be made,' the veil too embroidered with cherubim, the angelic guardians, of cunning work; the use, that the veil divided the holy place from the most holy, hanging between, so that the most holy, where the ark was, was hidden from view; the great type the commentators draw, that the veil shadows the flesh of Christ, through which alone we enter into the holiest, and which, rent at his death, when the veil was rent in twain, opened the way to God; that under the law the way into the holiest was not yet made manifest, the veil standing).

debates.question: What does the veil dividing the holy from the most holy typify?

sides.position: The flesh of Christ (Hebrews 10:20) — through which alone we enter into the holiest, and which, rent at his death (the veil rent in twain), opened the new and living way to God; under the law the way into the holiest not yet made manifest, the veil standing

themes: thou shalt make a vail of blue, and purple, and scarlet, and fine twined linen of cunning work
with cherubims shall it be made
the rich veil, wrought with cherubim, dividing the holy from the most holy
the great type of the flesh of Christ, through which we enter the holiest, rent at his death

schools.summary: Gill: the veil, being ten cubits long and as many broad, was hung upon four pillars of shittim wood overlaid with gold, with hooks of gold, set on four sockets of silver; the witnesses note God appointing the veil to hang upon four pillars of shittim wood overlaid with gold, with golden hooks, on four silver sockets — the supports of the veil, of gold upon silver (the veil hanging on golden pillars founded on silver: 'thou shalt hang it upon four pillars of shittim wood overlaid with gold: their hooks shall be of gold, upon the four sockets of silver'; the pillars, 'four pillars of shittim wood overlaid with gold,' four pillars of the incorruptible wood, clothed in gold, on which the veil was hung; the hooks, 'their hooks shall be of gold,' golden hooks by which the veil was fastened to the pillars; the sockets, 'upon the four sockets of silver,' that these pillars, like the boards, stood on sockets of silver, the foundation of redemption; the harmony of the type, the gold, glory and deity, upon the silver, redemption, the veil itself a type of Christ's flesh; that all that bore the veil was gold and silver, glory resting on redemption; that in God's house glory is founded on redemption).

themes: thou shalt hang it upon four pillars of shittim wood overlaid with gold: their hooks shall be of gold
upon the four sockets of silver
the veil hung upon four golden pillars set on four silver sockets
the gold (glory and deity) resting on the silver (redemption), glory founded on redemption

schools.summary: Gill: the veil was hung up under the taches or clasps which coupled the two grand curtains, and within it was brought the ark of the testimony; the witnesses note God appointing the veil to hang under the taches, the ark brought within it, the veil dividing the holy from the most holy — the placing of the veil and the ark, the two holy places parted (the veil parting the holy from the most holy: 'thou shalt hang up the vail under the taches, that thou mayest bring in thither within the vail the ark of the testimony: and the vail shall divide... between the holy place and the most holy'; the place, 'under the taches,' that the veil hung just under the golden clasps that joined the two great curtains, marking the division; the ark within, 'bring in thither within the vail the ark of the testimony,' that the ark was set in the innermost room, the most holy, behind the veil; the division, 'the vail shall divide... between the holy place and the most holy,' that the veil parted the tabernacle into two rooms, the holy place without and the most holy within; the great meaning the commentators draw, that the most holy, where God dwelt above the ark, was shut off by the veil, the way in not yet open, signifying that under the law access to God was barred till Christ, the veil of his flesh being rent, opened the way).

themes: thou shalt hang up the vail under the taches, that thou mayest bring in thither within the vail the ark of the testimony
and the vail shall divide unto you between the holy place and the most holy
the veil hung under the taches, parting the holy from the most holy, the ark within
signifying access to God barred under the law, the way not yet open till Christ, the veil of his flesh rent

schools.summary: Gill: the mercy seat was put upon the ark of the testimony, with the cherubim of glory overshadowing it, all a representation of heavenly things; the witnesses note God commanding the mercy seat to be put upon the ark of the testimony in the most holy place — the placing of the mercy seat over the law, where God met his people (the mercy seat covering the testimony: 'thou shalt put the mercy seat upon the ark of the testimony in the most holy place'; the placing, 'the mercy seat upon the ark of the testimony,' that the golden mercy seat, with the cherubim of glory overshadowing it, was set as a covering upon the ark, which held the testimony, the tables of the law; the place, 'in the most holy place,' within the veil, the innermost and holiest room, where God dwelt; the great meaning the commentators draw, that the mercy seat, the propitiatory, over the law signifies mercy covering the broken law, God meeting his people in mercy through the covering of the blood, a type of Christ the propitiation, in whom mercy and the law's demand are met; that God's throne, set over the law, is a throne of grace through the mercy seat; that all this was a representation of heavenly things).

debates.question: What does the mercy seat upon the ark of the testimony signify?

sides.position: Mercy covering the broken law — God meeting his people in mercy through the covering of the blood; a type of Christ the propitiation (Romans 3:25), in whom mercy and the law's demand are met, God's throne over the law made a throne of grace

themes: thou shalt put the mercy seat upon the ark of the testimony
in the most holy place
the mercy seat set as a covering upon the ark of the testimony, the cherubim overshadowing
mercy covering the broken law, a type of Christ the propitiation in whom mercy and the law's demand are met

schools.summary: Gill: the table of shewbread was not within the veil but without it, in the holy place, and the candlestick over against it; the witnesses note God appointing the table to be set without the veil on the north side, and the candlestick over against it on the south — the furniture of the holy place, the table and the candlestick set opposite (the holy place furnished: 'thou shalt set the table without the vail, and the candlestick over against the table... toward the south: and thou shalt put the table on the north side'; the table, 'the table without the vail... on the north side,' the table of shewbread set in the holy place, not the most holy, on the north; the candlestick, 'the candlestick over against the table... toward the south,' the golden candlestick set opposite, on the south side, facing the table; the arrangement, that the two stood facing each other across the holy place, the bread on the one hand, the light on the other; the meaning the commentators draw, that the shewbread, the bread of the Presence, and the candlestick, the light, shadow Christ the bread of life and the light of the world, the provision and the light of God's house, the sustenance and illumination of his people; that God furnishes the holy place with bread and light).

themes: thou shalt set the table without the vail, and the candlestick over against the table on the side of the tabernacle toward the south
and thou shalt put the table on the north side
the table of shewbread on the north and the candlestick facing it on the south
some seeing them shadow Christ the bread of life and the light of the world, the provision and light of God's house

schools.summary: Gill: the hanging was for the door at the east end of the tabernacle, which lay open and exposed, and for which a covering was needed; the witnesses note God commanding a hanging for the door of the tent, of blue, purple, scarlet, and fine twined linen, wrought with needlework — the screen at the entrance, of rich stuff, the way into the holy place (the door covered with a beautiful hanging: 'thou shalt make an hanging for the door of the tent, of blue, and purple, and scarlet, and fine twined linen, wrought with needlework'; the place, 'the door of the tent,' at the east end, the entrance into the holy place, which lay open and exposed and needed a covering; the materials, blue, purple, scarlet, and fine twined linen, the same rich colours as the veil and curtains, though wrought with needlework, a plainer work than the cunning work of the veil with its cherubim; the distinction, that the door hanging, though rich, had no cherubim, being the entrance, not the inner veil; the meaning the commentators draw, that this hanging was the door of entrance into the holy place, a type of Christ the door, by whom we enter into God's house and worship; that there is one appointed way in, beautiful and of God's making).

debates.question: What does the hanging for the door of the tent typify?

sides.position: Christ the door (John 10:9) — the one appointed entrance into the holy place, by whom we enter into God's house and worship; rich and beautiful, of God's making, though without the cherubim of the inner veil

themes: thou shalt make an hanging for the door of the tent, of blue, and purple, and scarlet, and fine twined linen
wrought with needlework
the rich hanging at the door of the tent, the entrance into the holy place
a type of Christ the door, by whom we enter into God's house, one appointed way in

schools.summary: Gill: five pillars of shittim wood for the hanging, one at each corner of the entrance and the others between, overlaid with gold, with hooks of gold, and five sockets of brass; the witnesses note God appointing five pillars of shittim wood, overlaid with gold, with golden hooks, on five sockets of brass, for the door hanging — the supports of the entrance-screen, of gold upon brass (the door hanging on golden pillars set on brass: 'thou shalt make for the hanging five pillars of shittim wood, and overlay them with gold, and their hooks shall be of gold: and thou shalt cast five sockets of brass for them'; the pillars, 'five pillars of shittim wood... overlay them with gold,' five pillars of the incorruptible wood, clothed in gold, to bear the door hanging; the hooks, 'their hooks shall be of gold,' golden hooks for the hanging; the sockets, 'five sockets of brass,' that these pillars stood, not on silver like the boards and the veil's pillars, but on brass, the meaner metal; the distinction the commentators mark, that the inner veil's pillars stood on silver, redemption, but the outer door's on brass, the brass befitting the outer court and entrance, as the goats'-hair and badgers' coverings were plainer without; that all is fitly ordered, each part on its proper foundation).

themes: thou shalt make for the hanging five pillars of shittim wood, and overlay them with gold, and their hooks shall be of gold
and thou shalt cast five sockets of brass for them
the five golden pillars of the door hanging set on five sockets of brass
brass, not silver like the inner veil's pillars, befitting the outer entrance, each part on its proper foundation
